name.text: Session 06 Final Practice Quiz — Mixed Format

intro.text: Practice exam for Session 06. 5 short answer + 2 long answer questions. 45 minutes. This quiz is for self-assessment — compare your answers against the model answers in final-practice-quiz.md.

questiontext.text:
A user reports receiving an email from ceo.office@acmecorp.support asking her to transfer $85,000 to a new vendor account. The company's legitimate CEO email domain is acmecorp.com. The email passed the company's spam filter.

Answer all four parts:

  1. What type of incident is this?
  2. What is the severity level (P1–P5)?
  3. Name two technical indicators that should be investigated.
  4. What is the one immediate action that takes absolute priority?

graderinfo.text:
Marking guide (4 points):

  • 1pt: BEC / Business Email Compromise / Social Engineering / Financial Fraud attempt
  • 1pt: P2 (High). Accept P1 if student correctly notes that if transfer completed = P1. Deduct 0.5 if P4 or lower.
  • 1pt: Any two of: email headers / originating IP, WHOIS of acmecorp.support, SPF/DKIM/DMARC results, other recipients of same campaign, sending IP reputation check
  • 1pt: Confirm user has NOT made the transfer / stop any in-progress transfer. Must be action to prevent the fraud, not just "open a ticket."

name.text: SA2: Cyber Kill Chain vs MITRE ATT&CK

questiontext.text: Explain the difference between the Cyber Kill Chain and MITRE ATT&CK. When would you use each in an incident analysis? (150–200 words)

graderinfo.text:
Marking guide (5 points):

  • 1pt: Kill Chain is a sequential 7-phase model (Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions)
  • 1pt: ATT&CK is a knowledge base of tactics and techniques based on real-world observations
  • 1pt: Kill Chain use case — executive/management communication, understanding attack progression stage
  • 1pt: ATT&CK use case — detection engineering, threat hunting, specific technique investigation, actor profiling
  • 1pt: They are complementary — mention that ATT&CK is more operational/granular, Kill Chain is strategic/narrative

questiontext.text:
You are investigating a ransomware incident. An IT team member says: "Let me just quickly restore from backup — that will fix it faster than an investigation."

Provide three reasons why this is the wrong approach, and describe the correct process in brief.

graderinfo.text:
Marking guide (5 points — 1pt each):

  • 1pt: Backup integrity unknown — backups may also be encrypted/compromised
  • 1pt: Scope unknown — other systems may be compromised; attacker still has access
  • 1pt: Evidence destroyed — forensic evidence lost; cannot determine root cause or prevent recurrence
  • 1pt: Correct process includes: isolate → collect volatile evidence → forensic image → verify backups → eradicate → THEN restore
  • 1pt: Mentions that restoring without eradication will result in re-infection

name.text: SA4: Chain of Custody — Definition and Importance

questiontext.text: Define chain of custody and explain why it matters. What happens to the evidential value of digital evidence when chain of custody is broken?

graderinfo.text:
Marking guide (4 points):

  • 1pt: Correct definition — documented record tracking evidence from collection through all transfers and analysis events
  • 1pt: Includes cryptographic hash (integrity proof) as a key component
  • 1pt: Broken CoC consequence — evidence may be inadmissible in legal/criminal proceedings
  • 1pt: Additional consequence — civil litigation, regulatory, or disciplinary proceedings may fail if evidence integrity cannot be proven

questiontext.text:
A threat hunter notices DNS queries for domains like:

a7b2c3d4e5f6a7b8.cdn.update-checker.com
f1e2d3c4b5a6f7e8.cdn.update-checker.com
9a8b7c6d5e4f3a2b.cdn.update-checker.com

All queries return NXDOMAIN. Answer:

  1. What technique is likely being used?
  2. What ATT&CK technique ID applies?
  3. Write a detection approach or query to detect this pattern

graderinfo.text:
Marking guide (5 points):

  • 1pt: DNS tunneling / C2 over DNS identified
  • 1pt: Explanation — high-entropy subdomain labels encode data; NXDOMAIN is expected behavior
  • 1pt: ATT&CK T1071.004 (DNS) and/or T1048.001 (Exfiltration over DNS)
  • 2pt: Detection approach — must include: (1) monitoring for high-entropy or long subdomain labels AND (2) frequency/volume of queries to the same base domain. Award partial credit for incomplete but directionally correct detection.

questiontext.text:
Your organization experienced a P1 incident: ransomware was deployed to 40% of Windows workstations after an attacker gained domain admin access following an initial phishing email. You are the senior analyst writing the Post-Incident Report (PIR).

Write the following PIR sections:

  1. Executive Summary (3–4 sentences maximum)
  2. Root Cause Analysis (at least 3 contributing factors with explanation)
  3. Detection Gaps (at least 3 specific gaps with proposed remediation)
  4. Recommendations (at least 5 specific, actionable recommendations with rationale)

Target length: 400–600 words

graderinfo.text:
Marking guide (20 points):

Executive Summary (4pt): Clear, non-technical, covers: what happened (2pt), business impact (1pt), current status (1pt). Max 4 sentences. Deduct 1pt if technical jargon makes it unsuitable for executive audience.

Root Cause Analysis (4pt): 1pt each for any of: phishing delivery, DMARC not enforced, no MFA on privileged accounts, no LSASS monitoring, flat network architecture, delayed detection. Must include explanation, not just label.

Detection Gaps (6pt): 2pt each for valid gaps. Full credit requires: gap name, why it wasn't detected, specific detection rule/event ID/query to address it. Partial credit (1pt) for gap identified without remediation.

Recommendations (6pt): 1pt each for 5 valid recommendations. Full credit requires: specific action (not "improve security"), owner assigned, rationale tied to a specific gap or finding. Accept any reasonable recommendations from the model answer or equivalent.

name.text: LA2: Threat-Centric vs Compliance-Centric SOC Analysis

questiontext.text:
You are mentoring a new SOC analyst who has been told to "just investigate what the SIEM alerts on" and to "close tickets quickly."

Write a response (300–400 words) that explains:

  • The core difference between compliance-centric and threat-centric approaches
  • Why the compliance-centric approach fails against sophisticated threats
  • Three practical techniques a threat-centric analyst uses that a compliance-centric analyst doesn't
  • A concrete example showing the difference in outcome

graderinfo.text:
Marking guide (16 points):

Core difference (4pt): 2pt for compliance = "did it match a rule?" / rule-driven. 2pt for threat-centric = "is this adversary behavior?" / intelligence-driven, focuses on TTPs not just IOCs.

Failure mode (3pt): Must explain that sophisticated attackers specifically avoid triggering compliance rules (LOLBins, valid credentials, below-threshold activity). Compliance-centric produces good metrics while attacker operates freely. (3pt for comprehensive explanation, 2pt for partial)

Three techniques (6pt, 2pt each): Accept any of: IOA/TTP-based hunting vs IOC matching, pivot and expand vs single-alert investigation, ATT&CK hypothesis generation for hunting, behavioral analytics vs signature-based, proactive threat hunting vs reactive alert processing, intelligence-driven detection engineering. Must explain HOW, not just name the technique.

Concrete example (3pt): Example must show a situation where compliance-centric misses a real threat that threat-centric catches. Must be specific (not just "compliance is bad"). Full marks for realistic scenario with clear outcome differential.
